kids.question: Who wrote the letter to the Philippians?
kids.explanation: Philippians 1:1 — "Paul and Timotheus, the servants of Jesus Christ, to all the saints in Christ Jesus which are at Philippi."

kids.question: Where was Paul when he wrote this letter?
kids.explanation: Philippians 1:13 — "my bonds in Christ are manifest in all the palace."

kids.question: What does Paul say is better than living?
kids.option_a: To depart and be with Christ
kids.explanation: Philippians 1:23 — "having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is far better."

teens.question: What does Paul say about the good work God began in the Philippians?
teens.option_a: He who began it will perform (complete) it until the day of Jesus Christ
teens.option_b: He started it but they must finish it
teens.option_c: He might abandon it if they don't cooperate
teens.option_d: He only started it in some of them
teens.explanation: Philippians 1:6 — "he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ."

teens.question: What happened because of Paul's imprisonment?
teens.option_a: The gospel spread even further — his bonds became known throughout the palace and others were emboldened to preach
teens.explanation: Philippians 1:12-14 — "the things which happened unto me have fallen out rather unto the furtherance of the gospel."

teens.question: What does Paul say about those who preach Christ out of envy?
teens.option_a: Whether in pretence or truth, Christ is preached — and he rejoices in that
teens.option_c: They will be punished by God
teens.explanation: Philippians 1:18 — "whether in pretence, or in truth, Christ is preached; and I therein do rejoice."

teens.question: What does Paul say is given to believers on behalf of Christ?
teens.option_a: Not only to believe on Him but also to suffer for His sake
teens.option_b: Only to believe and be happy
teens.explanation: Philippians 1:29 — "unto you it is given in the behalf of Christ, not only to believe on him, but also to suffer for his sake."

teens.question: What famous statement does Paul make about living and dying?
teens.option_a: "For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain"
teens.option_b: "I will live forever in this body"
teens.explanation: Philippians 1:21 — "For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain."

adults.question: What does Paul pray the Philippians' love would do?
adults.option_a: Abound yet more and more in knowledge and in all judgment (discernment)
adults.option_b: Simply increase in emotion
adults.option_c: Focus exclusively on fellow believers
adults.option_d: Lead them to abandon critical thinking
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:9 — "that your love may abound yet more and more in knowledge and in all judgment."

adults.question: What does Paul pray they would be until the day of Christ?
adults.option_a: Sincere and without offence, filled with the fruits of righteousness by Jesus Christ
adults.option_b: Wealthy and influential
adults.option_c: Free from all persecution
adults.option_d: Leaders of the Roman Empire
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:10-11 — "that ye may be sincere and without offence till the day of Christ; Being filled with the fruits of righteousness."

adults.question: What is Paul's dilemma between living and dying?
adults.option_a: He is in a strait betwixt two — desiring to depart and be with Christ (far better) but knowing it is more needful for the Philippians that he remain in the flesh
adults.option_b: He fears death and wants to live longer
adults.option_c: He is tired of life and wants to die
adults.option_d: He is indifferent to both options
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:23-24 — "I am in a strait betwixt two, having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is far better: Nevertheless to abide in the flesh is more needful for you."

adults.question: What does Paul exhort the Philippians to do regarding their conversation (conduct)?
adults.option_a: Let it be as it becometh the gospel of Christ — standing fast in one spirit, striving together for the faith of the gospel
adults.option_b: Follow Roman customs to avoid persecution
adults.option_c: Keep their faith private and hidden
adults.option_d: Separate into smaller house churches
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:27 — "let your conversation be as it becometh the gospel of Christ."

adults.question: What does Paul say about the adversaries of the Philippians?
adults.option_a: Their unity and fearlessness is to them an evident token of perdition, but to the Philippians of salvation, from God
adults.option_b: They will soon be converted
adults.option_c: They are secretly on the Philippians' side
adults.option_d: They are powerless against believers
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:28 — "in nothing terrified by your adversaries: which is to them an evident token of perdition, but to you of salvation, and that of God."

adults.question: How does Paul describe his affection for the Philippians?
adults.option_a: He longs after them all in the bowels (deep affection) of Jesus Christ
adults.option_b: He remembers them occasionally in prayer
adults.option_c: He is grateful for their financial support only
adults.option_d: He considers them one of many churches
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:8 — "God is my record, how greatly I long after you all in the bowels of Jesus Christ."

adults.question: What do the words "the praetorium" and "Caesar's household" tell us about Paul's imprisonment?
adults.option_a: He was imprisoned in Rome, and the gospel had reached even the imperial guard and household of Caesar
adults.option_b: He was in a local Philippian jail
adults.option_c: He was under house arrest in Corinth
adults.option_d: He was in a provincial prison in Greece
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:13 — "my bonds in Christ are manifest in all the palace (praetorium)." Also 4:22 mentions Caesar's household.

adults.question: What two motives does Paul identify among those who preach Christ?
adults.option_a: Some preach out of love knowing Paul is set for the defence of the gospel; others out of contention/envy, supposing to add affliction to Paul's bonds
adults.option_b: All preach from pure motives
adults.option_c: Some preach for money, others for fame
adults.option_d: Some preach from the Old Testament, others from the New
adults.explanation: Philippians 1:15-17 — "Some indeed preach Christ even of envy and strife; and some also of good will… The one preach Christ of contention… the other of love."

kids.question: Who does Paul say we should think of as more important than ourselves?
kids.explanation: Philippians 2:3 — "let each esteem other better than themselves."

kids.question: What did Jesus do even though He was God?
kids.option_a: He became a servant and humbled Himself
kids.option_b: He demanded to be treated as a king
kids.explanation: Philippians 2:7 — "made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant."

kids.question: What will every knee do and every tongue confess?
kids.option_a: Every knee shall bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord
kids.option_b: Every person will sing a song
kids.option_c: Every nation will build a church
kids.option_d: Every child will learn to read
kids.explanation: Philippians 2:10-11 — "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow… and every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord."

teens.question: What mind should believers have?
teens.option_a: The same mind that was in Christ Jesus
teens.option_c: The mind of a wealthy businessman
teens.option_d: The mind of a Roman senator
teens.explanation: Philippians 2:5 — "Let this mind be in you, which was also in Christ Jesus."

teens.question: What does Paul say about doing things without murmuring?
teens.option_a: Do all things without murmurings and disputings, that ye may be blameless and harmless
teens.option_c: Only leaders need to avoid murmuring
teens.option_d: Murmuring is a sign of passion
teens.explanation: Philippians 2:14-15 — "Do all things without murmurings and disputings: That ye may be blameless and harmless, the sons of God."

teens.question: How far did Jesus humble Himself?
teens.option_a: He became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross
teens.option_b: He humbled Himself only for a short time
teens.option_c: He humbled Himself only in appearance
teens.option_d: He humbled Himself only before the disciples
teens.explanation: Philippians 2:8 — "he humbled himself, and became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross."

teens.question: Who does Paul send to the Philippians and call a fellow worker?
teens.option_a: Timothy and Epaphroditus
teens.explanation: Philippians 2:19,25 — Paul plans to send Timothy and sends Epaphroditus back.

teens.question: What happened to Epaphroditus that caused concern?
teens.option_a: He was sick, nigh unto death, but God had mercy on him
teens.option_b: He was arrested by the Romans
teens.option_d: He was robbed on the road
teens.explanation: Philippians 2:27 — "he was sick nigh unto death: but God had mercy on him."

adults.question: What does the Greek word "kenosis" (from verse 7) mean in the Christological hymn?
adults.option_a: Christ "emptied himself" (made himself of no reputation) — not by losing deity but by adding humanity, taking the form of a servant
adults.option_b: Christ temporarily ceased being God
adults.option_c: Christ lost His divine attributes
adults.option_d: Christ gave up His throne permanently
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:7 — "made himself of no reputation (ekenōsen)" — He emptied Himself by taking servant form, not by losing divine nature.

adults.question: What does "being in the form of God" establish about Christ's pre-existence?
adults.option_a: The word "morphe" (form) indicates the essential nature/being of God — Christ existed in the very nature of God before the incarnation
adults.option_b: It means He looked like God but wasn't actually God
adults.option_c: It refers only to His appearance after baptism
adults.option_d: It means He was created in God's image like Adam
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:6 — "Who, being in the form (morphe) of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God."

adults.question: What is the result of Christ's exaltation?
adults.option_a: God highly exalted Him and gave Him a name above every name, that at His name every knee should bow in heaven, earth, and under the earth
adults.option_b: He returned to His previous state unchanged
adults.option_c: He became a powerful angel
adults.option_d: He received a new divine attribute
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:9-10 — "God also hath highly exalted him, and given him a name which is above every name."

adults.question: What does Paul say about working out salvation?
adults.option_a: "Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do of his good pleasure"
adults.option_b: "Work to earn your salvation through obedience"
adults.option_c: "Your salvation depends entirely on your efforts"
adults.option_d: "Once saved, no effort is needed"
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:12-13 — "work out your own salvation with fear and trembling. For it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do of his good pleasure."

adults.question: What imagery does Paul use about the Philippians shining?
adults.option_a: They should shine as lights in the world, holding forth the word of life, in the midst of a crooked and perverse nation
adults.option_b: They should hide their faith like a candle under a bushel
adults.option_c: They should only share their light with fellow believers
adults.option_d: They should wait until the world improves to show their light
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:15-16 — "ye shine as lights in the world; Holding forth the word of life."

adults.question: What sacrificial language does Paul use about his ministry?
adults.option_a: He says even if he is "poured out as a drink offering" upon the sacrifice and service of their faith, he rejoices
adults.option_b: He compares himself to the high priest
adults.option_c: He describes his work as building a temple
adults.option_d: He likens his ministry to farming
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:17 — "if I be offered upon the sacrifice and service of your faith, I joy, and rejoice with you all."

adults.question: What does Paul say about Timothy compared to others?
adults.option_a: He has no one like-minded who will naturally care for their state — all others seek their own things, not the things of Jesus Christ
adults.option_b: Timothy is the youngest and least experienced
adults.option_c: Timothy is equal to all others in devotion
adults.option_d: Timothy needs more training before he can serve
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:20-21 — "I have no man likeminded, who will naturally care for your state. For all seek their own, not the things which are Jesus Christ's."

adults.question: Why does Paul send Epaphroditus back to Philippi?
adults.option_a: Because Epaphroditus longed for them and was full of heaviness because they heard he was sick — Paul sends him so they may rejoice and he may be less sorrowful
adults.option_b: Because Epaphroditus was disobedient
adults.option_c: Because Paul didn't need him anymore
adults.option_d: Because the Philippians demanded his return
adults.explanation: Philippians 2:26-28 — "he longed after you all, and was full of heaviness, because that ye had heard that he had been sick… I sent him therefore the more carefully, that, when ye see him again, ye may rejoice."

kids.question: What does Paul say he counts as loss compared to knowing Christ?
kids.explanation: Philippians 3:8 — "I count all things but loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord."

kids.question: Where is the citizenship of believers?
What does Paul say he does — forgetting the past and reaching forward?
kids.option_a: He presses toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ
kids.explanation: Philippians 3:14 — "I press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus."

teens.question: What credentials did Paul list that he counted as loss?
teens.option_a: Circumcised the eighth day, of Israel, tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, a Pharisee, blameless in the law
teens.option_d: A degree from the university and published writings
teens.explanation: Philippians 3:5-6 — "Circumcised the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, an Hebrew of the Hebrews; as touching the law, a Pharisee."

teens.question: What does Paul want to gain instead of his own righteousness?
teens.option_a: The righteousness which is of God by faith in Christ
teens.option_c: A high position in the church
teens.explanation: Philippians 3:9 — "not having mine own righteousness, which is of the law, but that which is through the faith of Christ, the righteousness which is of God by faith."

teens.question: What does Paul want to know about Christ?
teens.option_a: The power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings
teens.explanation: Philippians 3:10 — "That I may know him, and the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings."

teens.question: Has Paul already reached perfection?
teens.option_a: No — he says he has not already attained or been made perfect, but he presses on
teens.option_b: Yes, he reached perfection years ago
teens.option_c: He claims to be close to perfection
teens.explanation: Philippians 3:12 — "Not as though I had already attained, either were already perfect: but I follow after."

teens.question: Who does Paul warn about as enemies of the cross?
teens.option_a: Those whose god is their belly, whose glory is in their shame, who mind earthly things
teens.explanation: Philippians 3:18-19 — "many walk, of whom I have told you often… that they are the enemies of the cross of Christ: Whose end is destruction, whose God is their belly, and whose glory is in their shame, who mind earthly things."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "the concision" versus "the circumcision"?
adults.option_a: He uses "concision" (cutting/mutilation) dismissively for those who demand circumcision, saying believers are the true circumcision who worship God in the spirit
adults.option_b: He distinguishes between two types of surgical procedures
adults.option_c: He refers to a geographical division between churches
adults.option_d: He describes a doctrinal debate about fasting
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:2-3 — "Beware of the concision. For we are the circumcision, which worship God in the spirit, and rejoice in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh."

adults.question: What does Paul call his former religious accomplishments?
adults.option_a: Dung (refuse/rubbish) — that he may win Christ and be found in Him
adults.option_b: A good foundation that Christ built upon
adults.option_c: Stepping stones toward salvation
adults.option_d: Valuable experiences he treasures
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:8 — "I count all things but loss… and do count them but dung, that I may win Christ."

adults.question: What does "being made conformable unto his death" mean?
adults.option_a: Paul desires to be so identified with Christ that he shares in Christ's pattern of suffering and death — dying to self and the world
adults.option_b: Paul wants to die on a cross literally
adults.option_c: Paul wants to experience physical death soon
adults.option_d: Paul plans to fast until death
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:10 — "being made conformable unto his death."

adults.question: What does "if by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead" express?
adults.option_a: Not doubt about salvation but Paul's earnest desire and determined pursuit of the full experience of resurrection glory
adults.option_b: Uncertainty about whether he is truly saved
adults.option_c: A conditional statement about earning resurrection
adults.option_d: Doubt about the doctrine of resurrection
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:11 — "If by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead."

adults.question: What athletic metaphor does Paul use for the Christian life?
adults.option_a: Forgetting the things behind and reaching forward, pressing toward the mark (goal line) for the prize of the high calling
adults.option_b: Swimming across a wide river
adults.option_c: Climbing a tall mountain
adults.option_d: Wrestling with an opponent
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:13-14 — "forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before, I press toward the mark."

adults.question: What does Paul say about those who are "otherwise minded"?
adults.option_a: God shall reveal even this unto them — showing confidence that God will correct their thinking
adults.option_b: They should be excommunicated
adults.option_c: They are lost and beyond hope
adults.option_d: They should start their own church
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:15 — "if in any thing ye be otherwise minded, God shall reveal even this unto you."

adults.question: What does Paul say the Saviour will do to believers' bodies?
adults.option_a: He shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto His glorious body, according to the working whereby He is able to subdue all things
adults.option_b: He will preserve them from all sickness
adults.option_c: He will make them invisible to enemies
adults.option_d: He will translate them immediately to heaven
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:21 — "Who shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto his glorious body, according to the working whereby he is able even to subdue all things unto himself."

adults.question: What rule does Paul give for spiritual progress in verse 16?
adults.option_a: "Whereto we have already attained, let us walk by the same rule, let us mind the same thing" — maintain unity and consistency in what has been achieved
adults.option_b: "Run as fast as possible to the goal"
adults.option_c: "Compete with one another for the highest prize"
adults.option_d: "Start over from the beginning with each new revelation"
adults.explanation: Philippians 3:16 — "whereto we have already attained, let us walk by the same rule, let us mind the same thing."

kids.question: What does Paul say we should do instead of worrying?
kids.option_a: Pray and give thanks to God
kids.explanation: Philippians 4:6 — "Be careful for nothing; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God."

kids.question: What does Paul say he can do through Christ?
kids.explanation: Philippians 4:13 — "I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me."

kids.question: What should we think about?
kids.option_a: Things that are true, honest, just, pure, lovely, and of good report
kids.explanation: Philippians 4:8 — "whatsoever things are true, whatsoever things are honest, whatsoever things are just, whatsoever things are pure, whatsoever things are lovely, whatsoever things are of good report… think on these things."

teens.question: What does Paul say the peace of God does?
teens.option_a: It passes all understanding and shall keep your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus
teens.option_c: It comes only after all trials end
teens.option_d: It is available only to church leaders
teens.explanation: Philippians 4:7 — "the peace of God, which passeth all understanding, shall keep your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus."

teens.question: What has Paul learned in whatever state he is in?
teens.option_a: To be content — he knows how to be abased and how to abound
teens.explanation: Philippians 4:11-12 — "I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content."

teens.question: What does Paul say God will supply?
teens.option_a: All your need according to His riches in glory by Christ Jesus
teens.explanation: Philippians 4:19 — "my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus."

teens.question: Who does Paul ask to help Euodias and Syntyche?
teens.option_a: His true yokefellow (companion), to help them be of the same mind in the Lord
teens.explanation: Philippians 4:2-3 — "I beseech Euodias, and beseech Syntyche, that they be of the same mind in the Lord. And I intreat thee also, true yokefellow, help those women."

teens.question: What does Paul say about the Philippians' giving?
teens.option_a: No other church communicated with him concerning giving and receiving except the Philippians
teens.option_d: He never accepted gifts from any church
teens.explanation: Philippians 4:15 — "no church communicated with me as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only."

adults.question: What does "Be careful for nothing" mean in Philippians 4:6?
adults.option_a: Be anxious about nothing — instead bring everything to God in prayer with thanksgiving
adults.option_b: Don't be careful about anything
adults.option_c: Don't worry about making mistakes
adults.option_d: Don't care about anything at all
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:6 — "Be careful (anxious) for nothing; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God."

adults.question: What six virtues does Paul list for the Philippians to think on?
adults.option_a: Things true, honest, just, pure, lovely, and of good report — plus virtue and praise
adults.option_b: Faith, hope, love, mercy, grace, and peace
adults.option_c: Strength, courage, wisdom, knowledge, power, and glory
adults.option_d: Prayer, fasting, giving, preaching, teaching, and healing
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:8 — "whatsoever things are true, whatsoever things are honest, whatsoever things are just, whatsoever things are pure, whatsoever things are lovely, whatsoever things are of good report; if there be any virtue, and if there be any praise, think on these things."

adults.question: What is the "secret" Paul has learned?
adults.option_a: "I know both how to be abased, and I know how to abound: every where and in all things I am instructed both to be full and to be hungry, both to abound and to suffer need"
adults.option_b: A hidden mystery about the end times
adults.option_c: A special prayer technique
adults.option_d: The location of a hidden treasure
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:12 — "I know both how to be abased, and I know how to abound: every where and in all things I am instructed."

adults.question: What does "I can do all things through Christ" mean in context?
adults.option_a: Through Christ, Paul can endure any circumstance — abundance or need, fullness or hunger — it's about contentment in all conditions, not unlimited ability
adults.option_b: Believers can do literally anything they wish
adults.option_c: Christ gives supernatural powers to all believers
adults.option_d: It applies only to miracle-working apostles
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:13 in context of 4:11-12 — "I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me" refers to contentment in all circumstances.

adults.question: What sacrificial language does Paul use for the Philippians' gift?
adults.option_a: "An odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God" — using Old Testament sacrificial terminology for their financial gift
adults.option_b: A tithe according to the law of Moses
adults.option_c: A tax paid to the Roman government
adults.option_d: A simple donation without spiritual significance
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:18 — "an odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God."

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "the God of peace shall be with you"?
adults.option_a: If they practice what Paul taught, God's presence (not just His peace, but He Himself) will be with them
adults.option_b: They will never face any conflict
adults.option_c: They will achieve inner tranquility through meditation
adults.option_d: Peace is guaranteed regardless of their behavior
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:9 — "Those things, which ye have both learned, and received, and heard, and seen in me, do: and the God of peace shall be with you."

adults.question: What does Philippians 4:19 promise and on what basis?
adults.option_a: God will supply all their need according to HIS riches in glory — not out of their resources but out of God's inexhaustible heavenly wealth, in response to their generous giving
adults.option_b: God will make them rich
adults.option_c: They will never lack anything material
adults.option_d: All prayers will be answered exactly as requested
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:19 — "my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus."

adults.question: What final greeting does Paul include that reveals the reach of the gospel?
adults.option_a: "All the saints salute you, chiefly they that are of Caesar's household" — the gospel had penetrated even the Roman imperial household
adults.option_b: All the saints in Jerusalem send greetings
adults.option_c: All the churches in Asia greet them
adults.option_d: Only Jewish believers send their regards
adults.explanation: Philippians 4:22 — "All the saints salute you, chiefly they that are of Caesar's household."
